text: Upon entry, dense adhesions of the bowel to the mesh are observed, with the mesh well incorporated into the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions using a force bipolar and a non-bowel grasping instrument, carefully avoiding direct contact with the bowel. The process takes approximately fifteen minutes, aiming to release the bowel from the anterior abdominal wall. The mesh is assessed and left in situ due to its incorporation and potential complications from explantation. The procedure progresses to retromuscular dissection.

The surgeon initiates dissection by recruiting the falciform ligament for posterior layer closure. The linea alba is visible and incised to enter the contralateral retro-rectus space. The retro-rectus dissection progresses, with the posterior sheath being grasped and the retromuscular dissection developed towards the patient's semilunar line. The right side of the retroactive space appears limited.

The surgeon begins dissection medially in the preperitoneal fat pad, reflecting the peritoneum and peritoneal fat pad downwards while elevating the posterior sheath of the rectus muscle. Transversus abdominis fibers are identified, and a small transection of one to two centimeters is performed. The remainder of the dissection is muscle-sparing, staying posterior to the transversus abdominis, following the Madrid modification technique.

The surgeon dissects to the semilunar line, incises the posterior sheath, and enters the preperitoneal space. A preperitoneal dissection is performed to create a larger space for posterior closure and mesh coverage. The dissection extends towards the retroperitoneum, revealing the retroinguinal space and trochars placed for contralateral dissection. The contralateral retrorectus space has more sheath, reducing the need for extended dissection.

The surgeon begins placating the patient's abdominal wall well above the hernia defect, using a number one permanent V-lock suture. The ventral hernia defect is closed by sequentially tightening the abdominal wall and placing it under traction, ensuring closure without excessive force.

The surgeon re-approximates the rectus muscle caudal to the defect and closes the cephalic component, achieving full reconstruction of the patient's linea alba. Mesh is placed prior to closing the posterior defect, followed by closure of the posterior sheath and visceral sac. A 26x28 mesh is used for broad coverage of the defect and retromuscular dissection.
